title: Ode on the Insurrection in Candia
content:
STR. 1

В В В В I laid my laurel-leaf
В В В В At the white feet of grief,
В В В В Seeing how with covered face and plumeless wings,
В В В В With unreverted head
В В В В Veiled, as who mourns his dead,
В В В В Lay Freedom couched between the thrones of kings,
В В В В A wearied lion without lair,
В В В В And bleeding from base wounds, and vexed with alien air.



STR. 2

В В В В Who was it, who, put poison to thy mouth,
В В В В Who lulled with craft or chant thy vigilant eyes,
В В В В O light of all men, lamp to north and south,
В В В В Eastward and westward, under all menпїЅs skies?
В В В В For if thou sleep, we perish, and thy name
В В В В Dies with the dying of our ephemeral breath;
В В В В And if the dust of death oпїЅergrows thy flame,
В В В В Heaven also is darkened with the dust of death.
В В В В If thou be mortal, if thou change or cease,
В В В В If thine hand fail, or thine eyes turn from Greece,
В В В В Thy firstborn, and the firstfruits of thy fame,
В В В В God is no God, and man is moulded out of shame.



STR. 3

В В В В Is there change in the secret skies,
В В В В In the sacred places that see
В В В В The divine beginning of things,
В В В В The weft of the web of the world?
В В В В Is Freedom a worm that dies,
В В В В And God no God of the free?
В В В В Is heaven like as earth with her kings
В В В В And time as a serpent curled
В В В В Round life as a tree?

В В В В From the steel-bound snows of the north,
В В В В From the mystic mother, the east,
В В В В From the sands of the fiery south,
В В В В From the low-lit clouds of the west,
В В В В A sound of a cry is gone forth;
В В В В Arise, stand up from the feast,
В В В В Let wine be far from the mouth,
В В В В Let no man sleep or take rest,
В В В В Till the plague hath ceased.

В В В В Let none rejoice or make mirth
В В В В Till the evil thing be stayed,
В В В В Nor grief be lulled in the lute,
В В В В Nor hope be loud on the lyre;
В В В В Let none be glad upon earth.
В В В В O music of young man and maid,
В В В В O songs of the bride, be mute.
В В В В For the light of her eyes, her desire,
В В В В Is the soul dismayed.

В В В В It is not a land new-born
В В В В That is scourged of a strangerпїЅs hand,
В В В В That is rent and consumed with flame.
В В В В We have known it of old, this face,
В В В В With the cheeks and the tresses torn,
В В В В With shame on the brow as a brand.
В В В В We have named it of old by name,
В В В В The land of the royallest race,
В В В В The most holy land.



STR. 4

В В В В Had I words of fire,
В В В В Whose words are weak as snow;
В В В В Were my heart a lyre
В В В В Whence all its love might flow
В В В В In the mighty modulations of desire,
В В В В In the notes wherewith manпїЅs passion worships woe;

В В В В Could my song release
В В В В The thought weak words confine,
В В В В And my grief, O Greece,
В В В В Prove how it worships thine;
В В В В It would move with pulse of war the limbs of peace,
В В В В Till she flushed and trembled and became divine.

В В В В (Once she held for true
В В В В This truth of sacred strain;
В В В В Though blood drip like dew
В В В В And life run down like rain,
В В В В It is better that war spare but one or two
В В В В Than that many live, and liberty be slain.)

В В В В Then with fierce increase
В В В В And bitter motherпїЅs mirth,
В В В В From the womb of peace,
В В В В A womb that yearns for birth,
В В В В As a man-child should deliverance come to Greece,
В В В В As a saviour should the child be born on earth.



STR. 5

В В В В O that these my days had been
В В В В Ere white peace and shame were wed
В В В В Without torch or dancersпїЅ din
В В В В Round the unsacred marriage-bed!
В В В В For of old the sweet-tongued law,
В В В В Freedom, clothed with all menпїЅs love,
В В В В Girt about with all menпїЅs awe,
В В В В With the wild war-eagle mated
В В В В The white breast of peace the dove,
В В В В And his ravenous heart abated
В В В В And his windy wings were furled
В В В В In an eyrie consecrated
В В В В Where the snakes of strife uncurled,
В В В В And her soul was soothed and sated
В В В В With the welfare of the world.



ANT. 1

В В В В But now, close-clad with peace,
В В В В While war lays hand on Greece,
В В В В The kingdoms and their kings stand by to see;
В В В В пїЅAha, we are strong,пїЅ they say,
В В В В пїЅWe are sure, we are well,пїЅ even they;
В В В В пїЅAnd if we serve, what ails ye to be free?
В В В В We are warm, clothed round with peace and shame;
В В В В But ye lie dead and naked, dying for a name.пїЅ



ANT. 2

В В В В O kings and queens and nations miserable,
В В В В O fools and blind, and full of sins and fears,
В В В В With these it is, with you it is not well;
В В В В Ye have one hour, but these the immortal years.
В В В В These for a pang, a breath, a pulse of pain,
В В В В Have honour, while that honour on earth shall be:
В В В В Ye for a little sleep and sloth shall gain
В В В В Scorn, while one man of all men born is free.
В В В В Even as the depth more deep than night or day,
В В В В The sovereign heaven that keeps its eldest way,
В В В В So without chance or change, so without stain,
В В В В The heaven of their high memories shall nor wax nor wane.



ANT. 3

В В В В As the soul on the lips of the dead
В В В В Stands poising her wings for flight,
В В В В A bird scarce quit of her prison,
В В В В But fair without form or flesh,
В В В В So stands over each manпїЅs head
В В В В A splendour of imminent light,
В В В В A glory of fame rearisen,
В В В В Of day rearisen afresh
В В В В From the hells of night.

В В В В In the hundred cities of Crete
В В В В Such glory was not of old,
В В В В Though her name was great upon earth
В В В В And her face was fair on the sea.
В В В В The words of her lips were sweet,
В В В В Her days were woven with gold,
В В В В Her fruits came timely to birth;
В В В В So fair she was, being free,
В В В В Who is bought and sold.

В В В В So fair, who is fairer now
В В В В With her children dead at her side,
В В В В Unsceptred, unconsecrated,
В В В В Unapparelled, unhelped, unpitied,
В В В В With blood for gold on her brow,
В В В В Where the towery tresses divide;
В В В В The goodly, the golden-gated,
В В В В Many-crowned, many-named, many-citied,
В В В В Made like as a bride.

В В В В And these are the bridegroomпїЅs gifts;
В В В В Anguish that straitens the breath,
В В В В Shame, and the weeping of mothers,
В В В В And the suckling dead at the breast,
В В В В White breast that a long sob lifts;
В В В В And the dumb dead mouth, which saith,
В В В В пїЅHow long, and how long, my brothers?пїЅ
В В В В And wrath which endures not rest,
В В В В And the pains of death.



ANT. 4

В В В В Ah, but would that men,
В В В В With eyelids purged by tears,
В В В В Saw, and heard again
В В В В With consecrated ears,
В В В В All the clamour, all the splendour, all the slain,
В В В В All the lights and sounds of war, the fates and fears;

В В В В Saw far off aspire,
В В В В With crash of mine and gate,
В В В В From a single pyre
В В В В The myriad flames of fate,
В В В В Soul by soul transfigured in funereal fire,
В В В В Hate made weak by love, and love made strong by hate.

В В В В Children without speech,
В В В В And many a nursing breast;
В В В В Old men in the breach,
В В В В Where death sat down a guest;
В В В В With triumphant lamentation made for each,
В В В В Let the world salute their ruin and their rest.

В В В В In one iron hour
В В В В The crescent flared and waned,
В В В В As from tower to tower,
В В В В Fire-scathed and sanguine-stained,
В В В В Death, with flame in hand, an open bloodred flower,
В В В В Passed, and where it bloomed no bloom of life remained.



ANT. 5

В В В В Hear, thou earth, the heavy-hearted
В В В В Weary nurse of waning races;
В В В В From the dust of years departed,
В В В В From obscure funereal places,
В В В В Raise again thy sacred head,
В В В В Lift the light up of thine eyes
В В В В Where are they of all thy dead
В В В В That did more than these men dying
В В В В In their godlike Grecian wise?
В В В В Not with garments rent and sighing,
В В В В Neither gifts of myrrh and gold,
В В В В Shall their sons lament them lying,
В В В В Lest the fame of them wax cold;
В В В В But with lives to lives replying,
В В В В And a worship from of old.



EPODE

В В В В O sombre heart of earth and swoln with grief,
В В В В That in thy time wast as a bird for mirth,
В В В В Dim womb of life and many a seed and sheaf,
В В В В And full of changes, ancient heart of earth,
В В В В From grain and flower, from grass and every leaf,
В В В В Thy mysteries and thy multitudes of birth,
В В В В From hollow and hill, from vales and all thy springs,
В В В В From all shapes born and breath of all lips made,
В В В В From thunders, and the sound of winds and wings,
В В В В From light, and from the solemn sleep of shade,
В В В В From the full fountains of all living things,
В В В В Speak, that this plague be stayed.
В В В В Bear witness all the ways of death and life
В В В В If thou be with us in the worldпїЅs old strife,
В В В В If thou be mother indeed,
В В В В And from these wounds that bleed
В В В В Gather in thy great breast the dews that fall,
В В В В And on thy sacred knees
В В В В Lull with mute melodies,
В В В В Mother, thy sleeping sons in deathпїЅs dim hall.
В В В В For these thy sons, behold,
В В В В Sons of thy sons of old,
В В В В Bear witness if these be not as they were;
В В В В If that high name of Greece
В В В В Depart, dissolve, decease
В В В В From mouths of men and memories like as air.
В В В В By the last milk that drips
В В В В Dead on the childпїЅs dead lips,
В В В В By old menпїЅs white unviolated hair,
В В В В By sweet unburied faces
В В В В That fill those red high places
В В В В Where death and freedom found one lionпїЅs lair,
В В В В By all the bloodred tears
В В В В That fill the chaliced years,
В В В В The vessels of the sacrament of time,
В В В В Wherewith, O thou most holy,
В В В В O Freedom, sure and slowly
В В В В Thy ministrant white hands cleanse earth of crime;
В В В В Though we stand off afar
В В В В Where slaves and slaveries are,
В В В В Among the chains and crowns of poisonous peace;
В В В В Though not the beams that shone
В В В В From rent Arcadion
В В В В Can melt her mists and bid her snows decrease;
В В В В Do thou with sudden wings
В В В В Darken the face of kings,
В В В В But turn again the beauty of thy brows on Greece;
В В В В Thy white and woundless brows,
В В В В Whereto her great heart bows;
В В В В Give her the glories of thine eyes to see;
В В В В Turn thee, O holiest head,
В В В В Toward all thy quick and dead,
В В В В For loveпїЅs sake of the souls that cry for thee;
В В В В O love, O light, O flame,
В В В В By thine own Grecian name,
В В В В We call thee and we charge thee that all these be free.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Algernon Charles Swinburne delivers a powerful performance in "Ode on the Insurrection in Candia"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
